item.title: From Multistability to Collapse: Rate-Induced Tipping of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation Under Linear and Periodic Freshwater Forcing
item.description: matched: multistability, Atlantic meridional overturning circulation, overturning circulation

item.title: SWOT High‐Rate Raster Data Reveals Antarctic Ice Shelf Motion and Change in Response to Ocean and Ice Dynamics
item.description:
matched: ice shelf, ice dynamics, Antarctic
Geophysical Research Letters, Volume 53, Issue 14, 28 July 2026.

item.title: Late Holocene stabilization of Conway Ice Ridge
item.description:
matched: ice sheet, ice stream, cryosphere, Antarctica, Holocene
Late Holocene stabilization of Conway Ice Ridge Andrew O. Hoffman, Paul T. Summers, Jenny Suckale, Knut Christianson, Ginny Catania, and Howard Conway The Cryosphere, 20, 3977–4003, https://doi.org/10.5194/tc-20-3977-2026, 2026 In Antarctica, fast-flowing ice streams drive most ice loss. Radar data from Conway Ice Ridge reveal that the van der Veen and Mercer Ice Streams were wider ~3000 years ago and narrowed progressively. Numerical modeling demonstrates that small thickness changes can rapidly alter shear-margin locations. These findings offer crucial insights into Late Holocene Ice Sheet readvance.

item.title: From ice to atmosphere: reviewing the coupled dynamics of heat fluxes in the cryosphere
item.description:
matched: cryosphere, glacier, meltwater, climate sensitivity
Abstract The interaction between glaciers and the atmosphere is generally recognized to affect melt rates, surface energy budgets, and even short-lived weather extremes in high mountain ranges. However, the Himalayan system remains hard to observe due to steep terrain and very few long-term data from the glacier surface. In winter, conditions in valleys such as Hunza or in the central Karakoram are not conducive to keeping instruments intact, increasing the uncertainty about how latent and sensible heat fluxes contribute to snow and ice melt, local moisture behavior, and temperature regimes. Recent observations in the field and model results indicate that turbulent heat exchange can drive glacier cooling and sublimation and can generate small-scale feedbacks that may interact with regional flow. At the same time, uncertainties persist linked to sparse measurements, debris-covered glacier surfaces, and a loose connection between atmospheric and cryospheric models in complex terrain. This review synthesizes current knowledge on turbulent heat flux dynamics in the Himalayas, with brief excursions into the Andes, Alps, and other cold mountain systems. The objective is to identify the key processes driving melt and to reveal the gaps that hinder confident projections of meltwater and climate sensitivity in High Mountain Asia.

item.title: Paleohydrologic conditions in the Mojave Desert during the last deglaciation recorded by core-to-shore lacustrine records
Local-scale variability in snow chemistry drives distinct microbial communities in Alpine seasonal snowpack
item.description:
matched: cryosphere, snowpack
Local-scale variability in snow chemistry drives distinct microbial communities in Alpine seasonal snowpack Anastasiia Kosolapova, Romain Castro, Ferran Romero, Catherine Larose, and Ianina Altshuler The Cryosphere, 20, 3959–3975, https://doi.org/10.5194/tc-20-3959-2026, 2026 Seasonal snow acts as a dynamic medium, accumulating atmospheric particles and microorganisms, creating unique microbial habitats in alpine environments. At the local scale, snow depth, not valley location, mainly drives chemical composition and bacterial community structure, while elevation predicts community differences across sites. Diversity drops within just 10–15 cm depth, reflecting distinct atmospheric sources and rapid post-depositional selection at centimetre scales.

item.title: Holocene vegetation change, climate variability, and human impacts on the Seki Basin (southwestern Türkiye): Evidence from a multiproxy approach
Spatiotemporal changes of soil profile salinization in Xinjiang and their response to glacial runoff variations
item.description:
matched: glacier, meltwater
Currently, the understanding of root-zone soil salinization—which critically influences vegetation growth—and its response to ongoing glacier retreat remains limited, hindering objective assessments of vegetation health and hydrological effects in arid regions. To address these issues, this study simulated profile soil salinization (0–100 cm) across the Mountain-Oasis-Desert System (MODS) in Xinjiang using a Random Forest (RF) model. Glacier runoff from 1960 to 2022 was simulated for multiple Xinjiang basins using the Open Global Glacier Model (OGGM), and the Geodetector model was employed to elucidate the response mechanisms linking soil salinization to glacial runoff and other environmental variables. Results show that soil salinization in Xinjiang exhibits a general increasing trend, characterized by surface accumulation (0–10 cm > 40–60 cm > 10–20 cm > 60–100 cm > 20–40 cm), although patterns differ between oasis and desert regions. Over the past 60 years, glacier meltwater runoff has increased in most river basins. In some basins (e.g., JSR, URB, ETNR), glacier runoff peaked before 2014, with the maximum value not exceeding 0.3 Gt. The largest annual glacier meltwater runoff occurred in the YarRB (4.48 Gt), followed by the WeiRB (3.66 Gt), the Ili River Basin (2.67 Gt), and the AksR (2.62 Gt). With increasing soil depth, dominant controls shift from climatic variables to soil physicochemical properties and topography, while the interactive effect between glacial runoff and other environmental variables becomes increasingly pronounced. Notably, glacial runoff exerts its strongest influence on the 60–100 cm layer, and this impact continues to intensify across basins, with an annual mean factor detection q-value reaching 0.63. This study is the first regional-scale quantitative investigation in Xinjiang to reveal the relationship between glacial runoff and deep soil salinization. These findings provide valuable insights for water resource management, salinization control, and ecological environmental protection.
